questions.name: JSON response
questions.question_text: Which method commonly reads JSON from a response object?

questions.name: Async await
questions.question_text: await can make asynchronous code easier to read when used inside an async function.

questions.name: Error handling
questions.question_text: A good UI response to a failed API request is to:

answers.text: Show nothing and hope it works later
answers.html: Show nothing and hope it works later

answers.text: Provide a helpful error state and recovery option
answers.html: Provide a helpful error state and recovery option

answers.text: To hide all delays from users forever
answers.html: To hide all delays from users forever

answers.text: To communicate that work is in progress and prevent confusion
answers.html: To communicate that work is in progress and prevent confusion

questions.name: missing-ok-check
questions.question_text: After const res = await fetch(url), a developer immediately calls const data = await res.json(). What is missing from this code?

answers.text: A check for res.ok — a 404 or 500 response resolves the Promise but res.ok is false, and calling .json() on an error response may throw or return unexpected data
answers.html: A check for res.ok — a 404 or 500 response resolves the Promise but res.ok is false, and calling .json() on an error response may throw or return unexpected data

answers.text: await should not be used with .json()
fetch() already throws an error for 404 responses automatically
answers.html: fetch() already throws an error for 404 responses automatically

answers.text: The response must be cloned before calling .json()
answers.html: The response must be cloned before calling .json()

questions.name: fetch-resolves-on-404
questions.question_text: A developer fetches a URL that returns a 404 status. The await fetch(url) call does NOT throw an error. Why?

answers.text: fetch() only rejects on network failures (no connection, DNS failure). HTTP error status codes like 404 resolve successfully — the developer must check res.ok
answers.html: fetch() only rejects on network failures (no connection, DNS failure). HTTP error status codes like 404 resolve successfully — the developer must check res.ok

answers.text: 404 errors are automatically retried by the browser
answers.html: 404 errors are automatically retried by the browser

answers.text: fetch() always throws for any non-200 status
answers.html: fetch() always throws for any non-200 status

answers.text: The try/catch block suppressed the error
